title: Short-Rate Models Compared

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-06-finance.md

setup.narrative: Four short-rate models: Vasicek, CIR (equilibrium); Ho-Lee, Hull-White (no-arbitrage). Compare on three axes: mean-reversion, non-negativity, and yield-curve fit.
rules: Vasicek: dR = a(b - R) dt + sigma dW (constant vol).
CIR: dR = a(b - R) dt + sigma * sqrt(R) dW (vol scales with sqrt(R)).
Ho-Lee: dR = theta(t) dt + sigma dW (no mean-reversion, time-dep drift).
Hull-White: dR = a(b(t) - R) dt + sigma dW (Vasicek with time-dep mean).
setup.question: Match each model to its key feature.

learningObjectives: Identify non-negativity properties (Vasicek violates, CIR preserves).
Identify which models allow exact yield-curve fit (Ho-Lee, Hull-White).

stages.label: Vasicek's flaw
stages.prompt: Vasicek: dR = a(b - R) dt + sigma dW. Why can rates go NEGATIVE under Vasicek?

options.text: The diffusion sigma * dW is INDEPENDENT of R, so even when R is small, the noise can push it below 0.
Vasicek's drift is positive, so R always increases.

stages.explanation: Constant vol means the diffusion doesn't shrink as R approaches 0. R follows a Gaussian distribution conditional on R(0), so P(R(t) < 0) > 0 for any t > 0. In low-rate environments this is empirically realistic but theoretically inconsistent for most economic models.

stages.label: CIR's fix
stages.prompt: CIR: dR = a(b - R) dt + sigma * sqrt(R) dW. How does this fix Vasicek's negativity issue?

options.text: Diffusion sigma * sqrt(R) -> 0 as R -> 0, so the drift dominates near zero. With 2ab >= sigma^2 (Feller condition), R stays strictly positive.
CIR uses log(R) instead of R.

stages.explanation: When R is small, sqrt(R) is also small, so the noise vanishes faster than the upward drift a(b - R) (which is large when R << b). Under the Feller condition 2ab >= sigma^2, R is strictly positive almost surely.

stages.label: Yield-curve fit
stages.prompt: Which models EXACTLY fit today's observed yield curve via construction?

options.text: Ho-Lee (theta(t)) and Hull-White (b(t)).  The time-dependent functions are calibrated to match every point on today's curve.
All four models fit the curve exactly.

stages.explanation: Vasicek and CIR are EQUILIBRIUM models with constant parameters -- they cannot exactly match an arbitrary observed curve. Ho-Lee uses theta(t); Hull-White uses b(t); these time-dependent components absorb the curve information. Hence 'no-arbitrage' models.

stages.label: Commit
stages.template: Vasicek allows __[a]__ rates. CIR uses sqrt(__[b]__) diffusion. Ho-Lee has no __[c]__ (mean-reversion / drift). Hull-White is Vasicek with time-dependent __[d]__ (volatility / mean).
stages.explanation: Quick reference: Vasicek (allows negative R), CIR (preserves R > 0 via sqrt diffusion), Ho-Lee (no mean reversion, fits curve via theta(t)), Hull-White (mean reversion + time-dep mean b(t), fits curve).

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why might a practitioner PREFER Vasicek's flaw (allowing negative rates) post-2014? (Eurozone, Japan have observed negative policy rates -- so Vasicek may match reality better than CIR there.)
What about volatility models for rates? Heston-style stochastic vol on rates (e.g., extended CIR or LIBOR market model with stoch vol) -- richer dynamics for caps/floors.
What's the LIBOR Market Model (BGM) and how does it differ? (Models discrete forward rates directly; arbitrage-free across the whole curve, ideal for caps and swaptions.)
